facts_disclosed: Looking at houses in Solihull — better schools for the kids

turns.content: do you know much about mortgages mate. specifically for self employed people

I know a fair bit about how mortgages work for self-employed applicants. It's definitely trickier than being on a salary, but it's far from impossible. What's the situation -- are you looking at buying somewhere?

yeah so me and Simran have been looking at houses over in Solihull. better schools for Arjun mainly, hes getting to that age where secondary school is on the horizon and the ones near us arent great. weve been on rightmove every night this week haha

Solihull's a great area for schools -- some of the best-rated secondaries in the West Midlands are there. And that Rightmove addiction is very real once you start looking, it takes over your evenings.

For a self-employed mortgage, here's what lenders typically want to see:

- **At least 2-3 years of accounts or tax returns.** This is the big one. They want to see a stable or growing income over time.
- **SA302 forms** from HMRC (your tax calculations) and your **tax year overviews.** These prove what you declared.
- **A decent deposit.** Most lenders want at least 10%, but 15-20% gets you much better rates.
- **Clean credit history.** No missed payments, no CCJs, manageable debt.

Are you a sole trader or limited company? It makes a difference in how they calculate your income.

sole trader yeah. been doing my own thing for about 8 years now. accounts are solid, I use an accountant and everything is proper and above board. my worry is that the banks see sole trader and just think its risky innit

Eight years as a sole trader with proper accounts is actually really strong. Banks do treat self-employed applicants with more caution, but 8 years of trading history puts you well past the "risky startup" threshold. Most lenders relax significantly after 3 years.

As a sole trader, they'll typically look at your net profit (what's left after expenses) averaged over the last 2-3 years. That's the figure they use to calculate how much they'll lend you.

A few things that work in your favour:
- 8 years of consistent trading
- Professional accountant handling your books
- Plumbing is a stable trade -- banks know demand for plumbers isn't going away

The key tip: **go through a mortgage broker, not directly to a bank.** Brokers know which lenders are self-employed friendly. Some high street banks are terrible with sole traders, but specialist lenders and building societies are much more flexible. A good broker will match you to the right lender.

What sort of budget are you looking at for Solihull?

thats good to hear about the 8 years thing. we've been looking at places around 280 to 320k. three bed minimum, ideally four cos the kids are getting bigger and they share a room at the moment. some of the four beds in Solihull are actually not bad for the price

£280-320k for a four-bed in Solihull is achievable -- there are decent pockets where you can get good value, particularly towards Chelmsley Wood, Marston Green, or the edges of Shirley. The closer you get to central Solihull the prices jump, but the surrounding areas still fall in the Solihull school catchments.

For a £300k property as a rough middle, you'd typically need:
- **10% deposit** = £30,000
- **15% deposit** = £45,000 (better rates)
- **Stamp duty** on a £300k first-time buy = £0 if you're first-time buyers, otherwise around £2,500
- **Legal fees, surveys, broker fees** = budget another £2,000-3,000

So you're looking at needing roughly £35,000-50,000 upfront depending on the deposit. How are you looking on savings?

thats the hard part innit. weve got about 20k saved which is decent but its not 45k is it. Simran earns as well which helps but the deposit is the mountain we need to climb. weve never bought before so wed be first time buyers which helps with the stamp duty at least

First-time buyer status is a big win -- no stamp duty on the first £425,000, so on a £300k place you'd pay zero. That saves you a couple of grand right there.

£20k is a solid start. Here are some options to close the gap:

- **5% deposit mortgages:** Some lenders offer 95% mortgages. On a £300k house, that's £15,000 deposit -- you've already got more than that. The interest rate will be higher, but it gets you on the ladder.
- **Shared Ownership:** You buy a share (usually 25-75%) and pay rent on the rest. Lower deposit needed but you don't fully own it.
- **Help to Buy ISA / Lifetime ISA:** If you or Simran have a Lifetime ISA, the government adds 25% on top of your savings up to £1,000 per year. If you haven't got one, it's worth opening one now even while you're still saving.
- **Family help:** Some lenders let parents put savings into an offset account to boost your application without gifting you money outright.

With two incomes and £20k already saved, a 5% mortgage at £300k is genuinely doable. A good broker would tell you in one meeting whether you'd get approved. Have you spoken to one yet?

no not yet. Simran keeps saying we should but I keep putting it off cos I think im scared theyll say no haha. 5 percent deposit is interesting though cos we could do that now. is the interest rate much worse on 95 percent

It is higher, yeah. Roughly speaking, a 95% mortgage might be around 5.5-6% versus maybe 4.5-5% for a 90% LTV at the moment. On a £285k mortgage (£300k minus £15k), that's roughly an extra £50-80 per month compared to putting down 10%.

But here's the thing -- you can always remortgage. Most fixed deals are 2 or 5 years. By the time you remortgage, your property has (hopefully) gone up in value AND you've paid down some of the loan, so your LTV improves and you qualify for better rates next time.

As for being scared of a broker saying no -- a good broker won't just say yes or no. They'll tell you exactly where you stand and what you need to do to get approved. If it's a no today, they'll tell you "save another £5k and come back in six months" or "here's what we need from your accountant." It's a roadmap, not a pass/fail.

Seriously, book a free consultation with a broker. Worst case, you know exactly what the gap is. Best case, you find out you're already good to go.

yeah youre right. Simran says dream big and shes usually right about these things. were just looking at the moment nothing serious but its nice to think about having our own place innit. the kids having a garden and that. Arjun could have his own room finally

There's nothing wrong with dreaming big, especially when the numbers aren't as far off as you think. A garden for the kids, their own rooms, a place that's yours not a landlord's -- that's a proper goal worth chasing.

And honestly, the fact that you're researching now is smart. Even if it takes another 6-12 months to pull the trigger, you'll know the area, the prices, and the process. When the right house comes up, you'll be ready.

Keep the Rightmove browsing going, book that broker consultation in the new year, and see where you stand. You might be closer than you think.

new year is a good shout yeah. weve got the India trip in december so january when were back makes sense. right thanks for all of that mate properly useful. I feel a bit less clueless about the whole mortgage thing now

You're far from clueless -- you've got savings, stable income, first-time buyer status, and two earners. That's a strong starting position. Enjoy the India trip, have a great December, and hit the ground running with a broker in January. Solihull might be closer than you think. Cheers.
cheers mate. ill let you know how it goes. Simran is gonna be buzzing when I tell her I actually looked into the mortgage stuff properly for once haha
Ha, tell her you did your homework -- she'll be impressed. Good luck with it all, and enjoy house hunting. It's stressful but exciting when you start seeing places you can actually picture living in.
